summary: Being here at the East Kentucky Social Club in 2023 is truly an honor. It started back when many of us from Lynch and Benham had to move away after the coal mines closed in the 1960s. Some men met in Cleveland and decided not to lose touch, which led to the creation of this club. Andrea Massey and her group have been instrumental in keeping it alive, especially after COVID paused our gatherings. It’s a special place where folks from Benham, Harlan County, and Lynch reconnect and remember our shared history.

Andrea Massey has been pivotal in organizing the East Kentucky Social Club events. Without her and her crew’s bravery to restart the club after COVID, many of us wouldn’t have had the chance to reunite. She’s very good at bringing together people from Benham, Harlan County, and Lynch, and I’m grateful she allowed me to come and speak with fellow community members. Andrea’s dedication has kept our community connected through the years.

Lynch, Kentucky, is where I grew up and where much of my family’s coal mining history took place. Back in the 1950s and 60s, it was a bustling coal town with several camps, but when Portal 31 closed in the early 60s, many families had to move away. My father worked at Portal 31 and later Portal 32, and my grandfather was a brakeman there too. Even after the mines closed, I stayed in Lynch while working for U.S. Steel and later the Department of Mines and Minerals. Lynch holds a deep place in my heart as the center of my family’s coal mining legacy.

Portal 31 was a major coal mine in Lynch where my father, grandfather, and great-grandfather all worked. It was active during the 30s, 40s, and into the 60s before it closed in the early 60s, which led to a big layoff and many families leaving the area. My grandfather worked there as a brakeman and even experienced serious injury on the job. Today, Portal 31 has been reopened as a tourist site, preserving the history of those days. It’s a symbol of our family’s deep roots in coal mining.

Portal 32 was another coal mine in Lynch where many younger men, including my father, were transferred when Portal 31 started winding down. It was located at the top of Lynch Mountain and represented the newer side of mining operations at the time. While the older men stayed at Portal 31, the younger workforce moved to Portal 32. It was part of the shifting coal industry landscape in the 1960s that affected many families in our community.

Benham, Kentucky, is where I grew up and went to school, and it was deeply affected by the coal mine layoffs in the late 1950s. I vividly remember the massive layoffs in 1958 and 1959 that devastated the community just before Christmas. Like Lynch, Benham’s economy was tied to the mines, and when they closed, many people had to leave to find new lives elsewhere. It was a tough time for families, but Benham remains an important part of my story.

Cleveland was the city where six men from Lynch, including some of my relatives, met unexpectedly years after the mines closed. They hadn’t been in contact for a long time and thought many had passed away, but this reunion sparked the idea to create the East Kentucky Social Club. That first reunion in Cleveland helped reconnect folks scattered by the coal mine closures. It was a meaningful moment that helped preserve our community bonds despite the outmigration.

U.S. Steel was the company that operated the coal mines where my family worked for generations, including Portal 31 and Portal 32. I worked summers for U.S. Steel during college, learning equipment operation and explosives handling, which helped me later underground. After college, I worked full-time for U.S. Steel as a roof bolter and other roles until 1979. U.S. Steel was seen as a stable employer, but I eventually moved on to the Department of Mines and Minerals for a long career in mine safety.

After graduating high school in 1969, I went to New York City for the summer to work in the Garment District. It was my first time away from home for any length of time, and it was quite an experience compared to life in Lynch. That summer job was a brief departure before I returned to college and eventually to coal mining. New York City gave me a glimpse of a different world before settling into my career back home.

The Garment District in Manhattan was where I worked during the summer of 1969 after high school. It was my first extended time away from Lynch and a very different environment from the coal camps. That summer job was a stepping stone before I returned to college and later the coal mines. The experience broadened my perspective, even though I ultimately chose to build my life back home in Kentucky.

entity: University of Virginia's College at Wise
summary: In 1972, I attended Clinch Valley College, which is now known as the University of Virginia's College at Wise. I played basketball there for a year while continuing to work summers for U.S. Steel. It was part of my college journey before I decided to leave school early and go full-time into coal mining. The college years were important for me, but ultimately the mines called me back home.

After working for U.S. Steel until 1979, I took a test and was hired by the Department of Mines and Minerals in Kentucky, which is now the Office of Mine Safety and Licensing. I started as a safety analyst and worked my way up to supervisor over a 30-year career. The training and skills I gained at U.S. Steel gave me an edge in mine safety enforcement. This job allowed me to stay in Lynch and continue serving the coal mining community in a new capacity.

Southeast Community College played a role in my grandfather’s life after he was injured in the mines. When he could no longer work underground, he was hired as a janitor at the college, where he stayed until retirement. The college opened around 1960 and provided a vital opportunity for him during a difficult time without unemployment or disability benefits. It’s a reminder of how important local institutions were to coal families.

My grandmother was born in Meridian, Mississippi, and moved to Kentucky when my mother was five years old. She came with her two brothers looking for a better life when the coal mines opened in Lynch in the 1930s and 40s. They saw Lynch as the promised land and moved here to improve their circumstances. Her journey from Meridian to Kentucky is a big part of my family’s story and the coal camp history.

My grandfather came from Alabama before settling in Kentucky. He met my grandmother, who had moved from Mississippi, and they married, blending their families. Alabama is part of our family’s roots, representing the migration of many coal miners and their families to the coal camps in Kentucky. It’s a key piece of the story of how our family came to be part of the Lynch community.
